text: The surgeon makes a utility incision in the fifth intercostal space and inserts an Alexis soft tissue retractor. The camera port is placed in the eighth intercostal space, positioned under visual guidance from inside the thoracic cavity, just above the diaphragm and anteriorly. The posterior port is also placed under visual guidance at the same height as the camera port.

The surgeon examines the lower lobe area, identifying a calcified lymph node with minimal stalk attachment. The node is removed without diathermy and sent for histological analysis. The area of abnormality is highlighted.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of the Articentral Wristed Instrument inserted directly through the utility incision. A silastic sling is clipped to the bed to stabilize the instrument's shaft at the bottom of the incision. Alternatively, the instrument can be secured through the plastic of the soft tissue retractor.

The surgeon uses a wristed Maryland instrument in the right hand to dissect and lift the inferior pulmonary ligament, exposing the area of abnormality. The dissection continues up to the inferior pulmonary vein. A wedge resection is planned for this area, and a lymphadenectomy is performed concurrently.

The surgeon uses the Articentral wristed instrument through the utility incision, demonstrating its ability to maneuver at various angles within the surgical field. The instrument is manipulated to push and prod tissues, resembling the movement of a robotic arm, while providing tactile feedback to the surgeon at the bedside.

The surgeon completes the wedge resection using the Maryland instrument with diathermy to address the remaining tissue. The resected area appears cystic and mucinous, suspected to be mucinous adenocarcinoma. The specimen is sent for histological examination, and preparations for a lobectomy begin.

The surgeon uses the artisanal wristed instrument to dissect parallel to the pulmonary artery, maintaining constant visual contact with the artery to avoid damage. The dissection reveals several small lymph nodes, including an R-Eleven I lymph node located between the middle and lower lobes.

The surgeon adjusts the plane of dissection using a wristed instrument, ensuring optimal safety. The camera angle is rotated slightly to provide a better view of the instrument's side, enhancing visibility during the procedure.

The surgeon gently dissects the sheath using a bipolar instrument, keeping it slightly open to ensure effective energy transmission. The dissection is completed with a hook instrument just beyond the pulmonary artery in a safer area. The segment six artery is visible in the distance.

The surgeon performs a meticulous lymphadenectomy, removing the R-Eleven I lymph node near the pulmonary artery. The middle lobe pulmonary vein is visible, surrounded by small lymph nodes, including an R-ten hyla lymph node. The surgeon examines the area under the azygos vein, identifying the inferior border as the definition for R-four lymph nodes.

The surgeon rotates the camera to a thirty-degree angle to visualize the posterior aspect of the lung. Using a sponge stick, the lung is brought forward to expose the station seven lymph node. The assistant dissects along the bronchus intermedius, identifies the esophagus, and centralizes the station seven lymph node before lifting and dissecting it off.

Adrian uses artisanal instruments to dissect around the station seven lymph node, with the bipolar energy generator set to thirty watts. The increased power causes visible sparks as the pleura is tested for the instrument's strength.

The surgeon checks the strength setting of the bipolar instrument while performing a lymphadenectomy around the station seven lymph node. The pulmonary artery is visible, and the camera angle is adjusted to achieve an optimal view for dissection under the artery.

The surgeon aligns a straight line over the bronchus intermedius to the middle lobe bronchus, then places a piece of black silk around the pulmonary artery. The pulmonary artery is prepared for firing, demonstrating the efficiency of surgeon-powered robotics in transitioning to the stapler. The vein is completed, having been prepped earlier.

The surgeon places a piece of string around the vessel and uses a stapler from the utility incision with a Covidien gold tip to secure it. The artery and vein are completed, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect out the lower lobe using an artisanal wrist instrument.

The surgeon identifies the inferior R-ten lymph node and follows the middle lobe bronchus as it curves under the lower lobe bronchus. The tissue surrounding the lower lobe bronchus is cleaned out, with careful dissection performed using a bipolar instrument at reduced energy settings. The surgeon skillfully adjusts the angle for precise tissue manipulation.
The surgeon uses a purple Covidien stapler through the posterior port to staple around the lower lobe bronchus, achieving a precise angle for effective tissue manipulation.

The surgeon performs an inflation check on the middle lobe bronchus to ensure it is not narrowed, with the middle lobe visibly inflating. The anesthetist inflates only the middle lobe, avoiding the upper lobe. The surgeon uses scissors to complete the dissection, and the resected lobe is placed into a bag through the anterior utility incision.

The surgeon resumes the station seven lymphadenectomy, utilizing a wristed instrument for enhanced angulation. The dissection proceeds along the bronchus intermedius and up the esophagus, aiming to visualize the left main bronchus. The sucker is positioned to touch the opposite main bronchus, indicating thorough lymph node removal.

The surgical team completes a full lymphadenectomy, revealing the esophagus, the back of the left atrium, and the carina at the top of the surgical field. The surgeon checks for perfect haemostasis in the area.

The surgeon performs a final dissection to retrieve additional R-IV lymph nodes, completing the lymphadenectomy to meet ESDS criteria for three N-II lymph nodes. An underwater check reveals a few small bubbles, prompting the use of a tissue patch to ensure no air leaks remain. The final view shows three incisions, indicating the completion of the procedure.
